title: Basic Security Awareness
subtitle: Five non-negotiable rules every developer must follow

sections.content:
You don't need to be a cybersecurity expert. But you absolutely need to avoid the mistakes that cause **most** security breaches.

The truth is that the majority of security incidents aren't caused by sophisticated hackers. They're caused by developers who forgot the basics: a password left in source code, user input that wasn't validated, an HTTP endpoint that should have been HTTPS.

These five rules will keep you out of trouble. They're not optional — they're the bare minimum for writing code that other people will trust.
Memorize these five rules. They cover the vast majority of security mistakes that junior developers make.
keyPoints: Never commit secrets: API keys, passwords, tokens → .env + .gitignore
Never trust user input: sanitize everything from forms or URLs before using it
Use HTTPS always: never HTTP for anything real — data in transit must be encrypted
Keep dependencies updated: run npm audit regularly to find known vulnerabilities
Don't log sensitive data: never console.log(password) or log tokens in production

sections.instruction: Spot the security problems in this code. There are three serious mistakes — see if you can find them all before reading the explanation.

blocks.label: Spot the bugs!

sections.explanation: Three problems: (1) API key hardcoded in source code — should be in .env, (2) Password logged to console — never log credentials, they end up in log files that others can read, (3) Password sent in URL query string and compared to a hardcoded value — URLs are logged by servers and browsers, and hardcoded passwords are trivially discoverable. Use proper authentication instead.

sections.question: Which of these should NEVER be committed to git?
sections.explanation: Your .env file contains secrets like API keys, database passwords, and tokens. These must never be committed to git. Add .env to your .gitignore file. The other files — package.json, index.html, and README.md — are safe and should be committed.

sections.prompt: To check for known vulnerabilities in your project's dependencies, you run `npm _____`.
sections.hint: This npm command scans your dependency tree against a database of known security issues.
sections.successMessage: Right — npm audit checks your installed packages for known vulnerabilities and suggests fixes.

completionMessage: You now know the five non-negotiable security rules. Never commit secrets, never trust user input, use HTTPS, keep dependencies updated, and don't log sensitive data. These aren't advanced techniques — they're the baseline that separates responsible developers from careless ones.
